phases.label: Default
phases.text:
1 — Arrival
 
> You've been on the road since dawn, and the forest has been with you for most of it — close, old, and quiet in a way that doesn't feel like rest. As the light begins to fail, the trees finally thin, and a village comes into view, tucked against the treeline. A few chimneys breathe smoke. A weathered post by the path reads: **Greywood Crossing**.
>
> It's the first sign of other people you've seen all day.
 
**Ask the table:**
 
- Before tonight — do you already know one another, or have your roads only just crossed?
- Take a moment and tell the table who your character is, and what brought them this far from anywhere.
*No checks. This is the beat where the party forms and introduces itself.*

phases.label: Default
phases.text:
> As you walk in, people stop what they're doing to look at you. Not hostile — but intent, weighing you, the way you'd look at someone you'd been waiting for without knowing it. A woman draws her child a little closer. An old man holds your gaze a beat too long, then nods.
>
> It's quiet. Too quiet for early evening. You get the sense that strangers haven't passed through here in a long while.
>
> One of them steps forward — broad, flour on his apron, the kind of man who either runs the bakery or just drew the short straw. He spreads his hands.
>
> *"Welcome, travelers. You'll want the Moody Mule — best roof in Greywood, and the only one worth the name. First round's on the house for guests."* He smiles. It doesn't quite reach his eyes.
 
**GM notes:**
 
- **If asked why the attention, or why the free drinks:** he deflects, warmly — they don't get many new faces, it's only good manners, a village likes to make an impression. Friendly, and a little too eager.
- **Social check (DC 2) — on a success:** the player reads it plainly. This isn't hospitality, it's *need.* The man is frightened, and he wants something from these strangers so badly he can barely keep it behind his teeth — but he won't say what, not here, not in the open.
- What he's hiding is **not** the bounty (that's posted openly in the inn). It's the shame underneath it: every group before them is dead, and the village has run out of its own people to send. Don't volunteer the job here — let them find the sign. This beat is about the *wrongness,* not the work.

phases.label: Default
phases.text:
> The Moody Mule is warm and low-ceilinged, the air thick with woodsmoke, tallow, and old ale gone into the floorboards. A fire snaps in a blackened hearth. It should be a comfortable room. It isn't, quite — the talk runs too low, the laughter comes a half-second late, and more than one pair of eyes keeps drifting to the door, as though the dark outside might decide to come in.
>
> A handful of locals are scattered across the benches. By the wall, a lean man sits alone with his back to the corner and a cup he isn't really drinking from, staring at nothing. Near the fire, a small, ancient woman watches you with sharp, unhurried eyes. A younger woman in the far corner hasn't looked up since you entered; the man across from her keeps a hand on her arm. A boy of maybe ten weaves between the tables, collecting cups.
>
> Behind the bar, a broad-shouldered woman is already pulling your drinks, her jaw set like she decided something about you before you'd said a word. Nailed to the board behind her is a notice, the lettering careful, as if someone wanted it to look official:
 
> **WANTED — THE BEAR**
>
> *To any adventurer or sword-for-hire brave enough: slay the beast that dens in the hills nearby, and Greywood Crossing will give 50 gold pieces — and a roof over your head, free, for the rest of your days.*
 
> She sets your drinks down without being asked.
 
**GM notes — the bear:**
 
- **At first**, the locals talk about it like a problem animal: a big bear, bolder than most, that's taken to killing anyone who goes into the woods. Bad luck. A dangerous season.
- **If the party presses** (questions, Social checks), the story comes apart. It's killed the village's *best hunters* — every group that went after it. None came back whole, or came back at all. The reward isn't generosity; it's desperation. Nobody goes into the forest now. Kendor is too far and too busy to send help.
- **They have a map** of where it dens, and press it on the party gladly. They warn that **goblins** infest the same hills — the party won't be the only thing in there.
- They can't, or won't, say much about the bear itself — only that it's *"not right,"* and *"clever, like it knows what you're thinking."* (That line does double duty: plain terror, or a hint at something that reads the Weave.)
- **Optional — Arcana or Intelligence check:** a character who knows Kendor's old stories recognizes what no one here will say aloud — that in this country, great bears are sometimes **Günduru**, Weave-touched and sacred, not to be hunted lightly. Half the village half-believes it, and is more afraid of being *right* than wrong. (Mechanically it stays a plain Great Bear — leave the ambiguity open.)
### The people here
 
**Maela — the innkeeper.** Runs the Mule, and the one who set the bounty's terms. Blunt, bone-tired, holding the village together by sheer will. Her brother went after the bear with one of the parties and didn't come back — which is why the reward is what it is, and why she'll look a sword-for-hire dead in the eye and not blink.
 
- Confirms the job, names the terms, and presses the map on the party. Warns about the goblins. Doesn't dress it up: *"I'm not going to tell you it's a fair trade. I'm telling you it's the trade we've got."* She won't beg — the desperation is in what she leaves unsaid.
**Cael — the survivor.** The lean man by the wall. The only one who walked out of the last hunt; the rest of his party didn't. He doesn't want to talk about it.
 
- **Coax him (Social check), or just buy him a drink and sit a while:** he gives the only firsthand account in the village. It's big — bigger than a bear should be, and *fast.* But what broke him wasn't the size. *"It was waiting where we were going. Not where we were. Like it knew the plan before we did."* He'll tell them the den lies deep in the hills, and that the goblins sit between the mouth and the bear — *"they're scared of it too. You'll see."*
- He won't go back. Don't let him be talked into guiding them; his use is the warning, not a fourth sword.
**Gisla — the old woman by the fire.** The village's keeper of old stories, and the only one who'll say aloud what the rest only fear. *(In-person route to the Günduru thread — she or the Arcana/Intelligence check surfaces it.)*
 
- **If approached:** a great bear in Kendor that hunts like this may be no animal at all — a **Günduru**, Weave-touched and sacred. *"You don't put a bounty on one of those. You set your house in order, and you pray."* She thinks the whole quest is a sin, and she isn't certain she's wrong. She offers no map and no tactics — only the weight of the choice.
**The room itself (ambient, no checks).** The silent young woman in the corner is a hunter's widow; her husband was in Cael's party. The man beside her is her father. The boy collecting cups is Maela's nephew — the brother she lost was his father. None of them will say any of this. It's just *there,* under the noise: a small village wearing its grief politely for the strangers who might, finally, be the ones to end it.
